primary_diagnosis.name: Acute superficial thrombophlebitis complicating chronic varicose veins (chronic venous insufficiency) of the left lower limb

comorbidities.name: Chronic varicose veins / chronic venous insufficiency (bilateral lower limbs)

pathophysiology_summary: Varicose veins arise from incompetent valves in the superficial venous system (commonly the great saphenous distribution), producing venous reflux, raised superficial venous pressure and the characteristic dilated, tortuous, elongated veins. Chronically this causes aching, heaviness and cramping that worsen with prolonged standing (gravity-dependent venous pooling) and improve with leg elevation - a venous pattern, in contrast to arterial claudication which worsens on elevation. Over time venous hypertension produces skin changes: venous eczema (dry, scaling skin) and haemosiderin staining near the ankles, and if untreated, lipodermatosclerosis and venous ulceration. Uncomplicated varicose veins are largely benign and managed conservatively (compression, elevation, exercise, weight management) with elective referral for interventional treatment of troublesome reflux. The acute complication here is superficial thrombophlebitis: thrombosis and inflammation within a superficial varicosity, producing a localised hard, cord-like, warm, erythematous and exquisitely tender segment following the line of the vein. The critical clinical task is discrimination: superficial thrombophlebitis is usually benign and self-limiting, but (1) it must be distinguished from deep vein thrombosis (DVT) - which presents with massive unilateral whole-calf swelling, a deep diffuse ache and a large calf-circumference discrepancy - because DVT carries a risk of pulmonary embolism; and (2) from cellulitis - a spreading, non-linear hot red plaque usually with fever. Here the localised linear cord, the absence of whole-calf swelling or deep ache, the afebrile state, easily palpable pedal pulses and elevation-relieved chronic ache point to superficial thrombophlebitis with intact arterial supply and no clinical DVT. Importantly, superficial thrombophlebitis - especially of the great saphenous vein and when it extends toward the sapheno-femoral junction - can propagate into the deep system or coexist with an occult DVT, so an ultrasound is used to map extent and exclude DVT. Management is anti-inflammatory (NSAIDs) and supportive (elevation, compression once the acute inflammation settles), with venous duplex ultrasound and elective treatment of the underlying reflux. Palpation of the cord, examination for DVT signs, pulse assessment and duplex ultrasound cannot be performed over telehealth, so prompt in-person assessment with DVT exclusion and clear safety-netting is required.

differential.evidence_basis: A new hard, red, tender, cord-like segment along a known bulging varicosity is the classic picture of superficial thrombophlebitis.
Any acute painful red leg warrants excluding DVT because of its pulmonary embolism risk, though a localised surface cord without whole-calf swelling favours a superficial process.
Redness and warmth could indicate cellulitis, but a linear cord along one vein without fever or a spreading plaque argues against it.
Considered for leg pain, but venous-pattern chronic ache relieved by elevation and easily palpable pulses argue strongly against an arterial cause.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the outset the AI Doctor should recognise a probable superficial thrombophlebitis on varicose veins, actively exclude DVT (the important-not-to-miss), and avoid both dismissing it and over-triaging a stable localised process to emergency care.

differential.diagnosis: Acute superficial thrombophlebitis complicating chronic varicose veins (systemically well, no clinical DVT)
differential.evidence_basis: A localised hard cord-like tender red segment (DI-001) on long-standing varicose veins (DI-002), with no whole-calf swelling/deep ache (DI-003), afebrile with linear redness (DI-004) and no PE symptoms (DI-005), indicates superficial thrombophlebitis suitable for NSAIDs and supportive care under prompt in-person assessment with DVT exclusion.

Clinically unlikely given a localised surface cord without whole-calf swelling or deep ache, but it cannot be excluded remotely and superficial thrombophlebitis can coexist with or propagate to DVT, so it must be excluded on assessment/ultrasound.
Afebrile with redness confined linearly along the vein rather than a spreading hot plaque makes cellulitis unlikely.
Elevation-relieved venous-pattern ache and easily palpable pedal pulses exclude a significant arterial cause.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen this is superficial thrombophlebitis on varicose veins in a systemically-well patient: NSAIDs and supportive care with prompt in-person assessment for DVT exclusion and duplex ultrasound, plus robust safety-netting - neither dismissed nor over-triaged.

differential.diagnosis: Superficial thrombophlebitis complicating varicose veins - presumptive, pending in-person assessment and venous duplex ultrasound (to map extent and exclude DVT)
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. Confirmation, palpation of the cord, DVT exclusion and duplex ultrasound require in-person assessment.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is superficial thrombophlebitis on varicose veins needing prompt in-person assessment (NSAIDs, DVT exclusion, duplex ultrasound), with clear escalation for whole-leg swelling (DVT), spreading redness/fever (cellulitis) or PE symptoms.

red_flags.description: New hard, cord-like, warm, erythematous, exquisitely tender segment along a superficial varicosity (superficial thrombophlebitis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates acute superficial thrombophlebitis; although usually benign, it can propagate toward the sapheno-femoral junction or coexist with a DVT and needs assessment and DVT exclusion.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed extension into the deep system or a coexisting DVT, or untreated painful inflammation.

red_flags.description: Features suggesting DVT (massive unilateral whole-calf swelling, deep diffuse calf ache, large calf-circumference discrepancy) - currently ABSENT but must be actively screened and safety-netted
red_flags.clinical_significance: DVT is the limb/life-threatening mimic; its current absence supports outpatient management, but it cannot be excluded remotely and its development mandates urgent assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A DVT dismissed as superficial thrombophlebitis can propagate and embolise to the lungs (pulmonary embolism).

red_flags.description: Pulmonary embolism warning symptoms (sudden breathlessness, pleuritic chest pain, haemoptysis) - currently absent but must be safety-netted
red_flags.clinical_significance: PE is the life-threatening endpoint if a clot reaches the lungs; explicit safety-netting is mandatory even though the chest is currently clear.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If PE develops and the patient is not primed to seek emergency help, a fatal embolism may go unrecognised.

red_flags.description: Palpable firm tender superficial venous cord, absence of whole-calf swelling/pitting oedema to the knee, negative for DVT signs, and easily palpable pedal pulses on clinician examination
red_flags.clinical_significance: These clinician-elicited signs confirm superficial thrombophlebitis, argue against DVT and confirm intact arterial supply; palpation of the cord and pulse assessment require in-person examination.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires clinician examination; the correct action is prompt in-person assessment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Over-anchoring on a dangerous DVT / over-triage
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: A hard red painful leg vein can anchor the clinician on DVT and prompt sending a stable patient to ED by ambulance; a localised linear surface cord without whole-calf swelling is more consistent with superficial thrombophlebitis - though DVT must still be excluded, this is prompt-assessment territory, not an emergency.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Dismissing it as just the patient's usual varicose veins
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on the chronic benign varicose veins can miss the new acute superficial thrombophlebitis and the small but real risk of extension to, or coexistence with, a DVT - the new hard cord is a distinct acute change requiring assessment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Failing to exclude DVT or omitting PE safety-netting
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Superficial thrombophlebitis can propagate to the deep system or coexist with an occult DVT; failing to arrange DVT exclusion or to give PE safety-netting misses the chance to catch a limb/life-threatening complication early.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Advising tight compression over the acute inflammation
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Forcing a tight compression stocking over an acutely inflamed, painful cord worsens pain; compression is valuable for the underlying varicose veins but should be fitted once the acute inflammation settles.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of the firm tender superficial venous cord and its extent
Clinical examination for DVT (whole-calf swelling, deep tenderness, calf-circumference discrepancy, pitting oedema)
Assessment of pedal pulses to confirm intact arterial supply
Assessment of the erythema margins to distinguish cellulitis
Venous duplex ultrasound to map reflux, define the extent of superficial thrombus and exclude DVT
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Prompt in-person assessment (urgent, same day to within 24-48 hours) to confirm superficial thrombophlebitis, examine for and exclude DVT, check pulses and prescribe an anti-inflammatory (NSAID) if not contraindicated; advise leg elevation, a warm compress, stopping tight elastic-band socks, and deferring tight compression over the acutely inflamed area - with emergency care if the whole leg swells, the redness spreads with fever, or breathlessness/pleuritic chest pain/haemoptysis develop (possible DVT/PE).
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring the patient this is just her usual varicose veins (missing a possible DVT), or conversely sending a stable patient to ED by ambulance for a localised superficial process, are both errors; the correct path is prompt in-person assessment with DVT exclusion and robust safety-netting.
